segments.s239-g121: دلم is the noun دل plus the enclitic -م (my), so literally “my heart.” شادمان شد is a predicative adjective + past of شدن (شادمان 'joyful' + شد 'became'), giving “became joyful” (simple past). ز here is the causal preposition “because of / at,” and پولادوند is the name (poetic/epic form derived from پولاد, ‘steel’) giving the cause: “my heart became joyful because of Polādvand.”
segments.s239-g122: که introduces a relative clause referring back to پولادوند (“who”). بفزود is an archaic/epic past form of فزودن (with the prefixed ب-), third person singular, “he added.” بر is the preposition “on/upon/to” marking the target of addition, and بند پولاد بند is a poetic phrasing meaning a steel link/fetter (پولاد modifies بند); the clause says he added a steel link onto/onto the steel bond/chain.
segments.s239-g89: همی بود is an archaic/progressive past construction: همی (habitual/continuous marker) + بود (past ‘was’) = 'remained/was for a time'. یک هفته gives the duration 'for a week'. با می‌بدست is a prepositional phrase 'with mey (wine) in hand' — می (wine) + بدست (in hand/by hand), here idiomatically 'with a cup/wine in hand'.
segments.s239-g90: ازو here is 'of/for him' (i.e., 'on his account' / 'about him') and shadman is an adjective/predicate 'glad, rejoicing' describing the people or the occasion. تاج و تخت are coordinated objects 'crown and throne' (و links them), and نشست is the perfective past 'sat' (he/she/they sat or he was seated); the clause reads as a compact sequence: 'rejoicing, (he) took the crown and the throne and sat.'
segments.s239-g91: سخنهای رستم is a genitive phrase 'the words / praises of Rostam' (سخنهای = 'utterances/words/praises'). بنای here functions as a nominalized notion of 'beginning/intention' or 'a start', followed by و برود where برود is an older-form verb 'goes/left' (3rd-person imperfective/optative in epic style). The construction therefore conveys 'the talk about Rostam began and (then) departed/ceased/moved on.'
segments.s239-g92: بگفتند is a past-tense reporting verb 'they said/they sang/it was said'. بر here indicates direction/target ('about' or 'to/toward') and پهلوانی refers to the heroic subject (used here as the object/topic: 'regarding the hero'). سرود is an object meaning 'a song/ode' — together: 'they recited/sang an ode in honor of the hero.' The verb first (بگفتند) is typical of narrative word-order in the Shahnameh.
segments.s239-g93: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmatan). بیک ماه expresses a duration 'for one month' where بیک is an older fused form (بیِ یک / بِ یک) meaning 'for one' and ماه is 'month'; together they give the temporal span. نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king' (نزدیک placed before the noun it modifies).
segments.s239-g94: همی بود is again the continuous/epic past 'he remained/was'. با جام is 'with a cup' (instrumental/attendant phrase: holding a cup). در پیشگاه is a locative phrase 'in the presence (of the court/king)' — together: 'he remained with a cup in the royal presence.'
segments.s239-g95: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that / thereafter'. چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); با شهریار places the addressee 'to/with the king' (با + title). The verb گفت introduces direct speech addressed to the شهریار.
segments.s239-g96: که‌ای here is a vocative/exclamatory combination (که + ای) used to address someone: 'O you...'. The adjectives پرهنر (learned/skilled), نامور (renowned), تاجدار (crowned/sovereign) are stacked epithets describing the addressee; the sequence is a formal honorific address to the king.
segments.s239-g41: بفرمود تا + subordinate clause: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here functions as a complementizer 'that' introducing the command. پیلتن is the name/place that is the logical object of the order, and برنشست is the past tense verb 'sat upon / took a seat' (بر‑ prefix + نشست). Word order is Verb (بفرمود) + تا + complement (پیلتن برنشست).
segments.s239-g42: This is a participial clause describing what was done next: گرفته is a perfective participle 'having taken/seized', and it governs the following phrase. همه راه = 'the whole way' (i.e. all the way/throughout the way). دستش بدست is an idiomatic reductive phrase meaning 'his hand in (one's) hand' → 'taking him by the hand' or 'hand in hand'. Together the clause means 'having taken him by the hand, (he) led him all the way.'
segments.s239-g43: همی گفت is a frequentive or continuous past 'he kept saying' (همی gives continuous/iterative aspect). چندین چرا ماندی is a direct question 'why did you remain/linger so long?' — چندین here intensifies duration ('for many moments/several [times]'), چرا = 'why', and ماندی is the past/preterite 2nd/3rd-person form 'you/he remained/lingered'. The clause is direct speech framed by همی گفت.
segments.s239-g44: که introduces a subordinate expository clause 'that/which' or a complement to the question. بر ما = 'upon us/against us' (بر as 'upon'), همی again gives iterative/continuous force to the verb, آتش = 'fire', and افشاندی is the 2nd/3rd-person past of افشانیدن 'you/he scattered/strewn/let loose' (here 'you flung' or 'you poured out'). The whole clause reads 'that you kept flinging fire upon us.'
segments.s239-g45: چو at the start is a simile/associative particle 'as/like' or a coordinating 'when', here used to introduce a list. The following words are a series of proper names joined by و 'and' (طوس, فریبرز, گودرز, گیو). This segment is simply a coordinated list of heroes following چو.
segments.s239-g46: Another parallel clause beginning with چو 'as' followed by more names. رهام, گرگین are proper names joined by و, and گردان here functions as a noun meaning 'troop/unit' modifying the following item نیو. The whole phrase lists another group: 'as Roham and Gorgin and the troop of ...'.
segments.s239-g47: ز ره سوی is a compact poetic sequence: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from', ره = 'way/road', سوی = 'toward/to' — together 'from the road toward'. ایوان شاه = 'the king's iwan/porch'. آمدند is the plural past 'they came'. Word order is prepositional phrase + place + verb 'they came toward the king's iwan.'
segments.s239-g48: بدان here functions as the combination ب + آن (poetic 'to that'), so 'to that/into that'. نامور = 'renowned', بارگاه = 'court/palace', and آمدند repeats 'they came'. The clause restates arrival 'they came to that renowned palace.' Grammatically, this is a short adverbial/prepositional phrase + noun + verb.
segments.s239-g33: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو (here = “when”) placed after the subject: تهمتن (the epic epithet/name) + چو + direct object تَاج سرافراز + past verb دید. دید is simple past (he saw). Word order is subject – conjunction – object – verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s239-g34: Main clause: object-augmenting phrase عالمی/جهانی + adverb سراسر (throughout, 'all over') + adjective/compound پرآواز (here 'resounding/filled with sound/acclaim') + past verb دید. The verb دید repeats to emphasize two perceptions (he saw X and he saw Y); adjective phrase modifies the following understood noun 'world'.
segments.s239-g35: Compound past actions joined by و: فرود آمد (he alighted/came down) + و + برد + پیشش نماز. برد here is a past verb meaning 'carried/brought' but the provided sense list lacks that meaning (see senses). پیشش ('before him/in his presence') modifies نماز (the ritual/prayer) as the object being presented.
segments.s239-g36: Inversion typical of poetry: the verb بپرسید (asked) comes first, then the subject/agent خسرو (here the proper name Khosrow), and the prepositional phrase ز راه دراز ('from/of the long road') follows. The verb is simple past (he asked).
segments.s239-g37: Compact past action: گرفتَش (he seized him) + بآغوش (into the arms/in embrace) + در شاه تَنگ (literally 'in the king tight/close') — here در marks location 'in' and تنگ describes the embrace or closeness. Word order places the verbal predicate first then spatial complements.
segments.s239-g38: Transition/temporal clause: چنین 'thus' + تا 'until' + برآمد 'arose/occurred' + زمانی 'for a while' + درنگ 'a pause/delay'. Together they mean 'thus, until some time passed, there was a pause' — تا here marks the temporal limit and برآمد is past tense.
segments.s239-g39: Present/habitual adverbial همی ('still/continuously' here giving the action ongoing force) + آفرین (an acclamation 'bravo') + خواند (he cried/uttered). شاه جهان is the subject 'king of the world' with خواند governing the reported cry. خواند is simple past 'he uttered/called out.'
segments.s239-g40: Short prepositional/identifying phrase: بران ('on that/to that') points toward the following referents, نامور (renowned) modifies موبد (the mōbed/priest) and پهلوان (hero/champion) follows joined by و. The phrase identifies whom the king praised.
segments.s239-g81: چنان شاد شد زان سخن تاجور is a compact clause: چنان شاد شد = “became so happy,” زان سخن = “because of that speech” (زان = poetic form of از + آن = “from/that”), تاجور apposes سخن as its possessor/agent (“the merchant’s speech”). Past tense شد (became) describes a completed emotional change.
segments.s239-g82: که introduces a result clause: “that/so that …”; گفتی here functions as a formulaic parenthetical meaning roughly “as is said / as you (have) said” (classical usage), followed by ز ایوان ‘from the portico’; برآورد سر is a compound verbal phrase meaning “he raised/lifted his head” (برآورد is the perfective verb governing سر).
segments.s239-g83: چنین داد پاسخ = “(he) gave such an answer / thus replied”: چنین (thus) modifies داد پاسخ (gave an answer). که‌ای پهلوان is the start of the quoted address; که‌ای here is the vocative particle (که + ای) introducing the direct address “O champion.”
segments.s239-g84: توی پیر و بیدار و روشن‌روان is a direct address describing the addressee: توی (older/poetic spelling of توئی) = “you are,” followed by three adjectives in coordinate order — پیر (old), بیدار (awake/alert), روشن‌روان (bright‑souled / clear‑minded). The adjectives are simple predicates that follow the copular/to-be form.
segments.s239-g85: کسی کش خرد باشد آموزگار reads as an optative/injunctive clause: کسی (someone) + کش (agentive/relational element attached to indicate a person characterized by the following noun) + خرد (wisdom) = “one characterized by wisdom / a man of wisdom.” باشد is optative/subjunctive here (“let him be”), and آموزگار is the predicate noun “a teacher.”
segments.s239-g86: نگه داردش گردش روزگار is a separate clause meaning “may the rotation/turning of the world keep him (in view/protection).” نگه (keeping/watch) + داردش (has/holds him) is a verbal periphrasis; گردش روزگار = “the turning/cycle of time/fate” is the subject causing the keeping. The overall mood is hortative/optative (a wish for preservation).
segments.s239-g87: ازین پهلوان چشم بد دور باد is an optative wish: ازین پهلوان = “from this champion” (ازین = poetic from/of this), چشم بد = “evil eye”/“evil gaze,” دور = “far,” and باد is the optative particle (may it be). Word order places the thing wished away (چشم بد) before the stative predicate (دور باد).
segments.s239-g88: همه زندگانیش در سور باد is another optative clause: همه زندگانیش = “all his life” (همه + زندگانی/زندگانیش), در سور = “in/at a feast” (سور = banquet/feast), and باد again is the optative particle meaning “may it be.” The structure expresses a wish that his whole life be spent in celebration.
segments.s239-g97: This is a nominal phrase describing the ruler: جهاندار 'world‑ruler' followed by a 'با' phrase that lists two qualities. The preposition با + nouns (دانش و نیک‌خوست) functions like an English 'endowed with / having', and there is an implied copula (is) omitted in Persian poetic style (i.e. 'the ruler is learned and benevolent').
segments.s239-g98: ولیکن is a conjunctive adversative 'but'. The construction مرا ... آرزوست is a dative-experiencer pattern: 'مرا' (to me) indicates the experiencer, چهر زال is the thing desired (object, marked by را), and آرزوست is the predicate 'is a desire' (آرزو + -ست copula). Word order is archaic/poetic: literally 'but to me the face of Zal is a desire' = 'I long for Zal's face.'
segments.s239-g99: در گنج is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/inside the treasure (vault)'. بگشاد is a past tense verb 'opened' (archaic/poetic preform b‑ + root گشاد), and شاه جهان 'the king of the world' is the subject; note the subject follows the verb here, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s239-g100: ز پرمایه چیزی is a noun phrase 'something of abundant/substantial worth' (ز = 'of/from' used poetically). که بودش نهان is a relative clause 'which was hidden': که = 'which/that', بودش = 'was it' (enclitic -ش referring back to چیزی; older word order places the verb before the predicate), نهان = 'hidden'.
segments.s239-g101: This is a poetic list introduced by the preposition ز ('of' / 'from'). The sequence names valuables: یاقوت 'ruby', then a rhythmic/compound form وز before تاج which here functions as a poetic linker (see note) and انگشتری 'ring'. The items are coordinated; punctuation is omitted as is common in lists.
segments.s239-g102: Another 'ز' phrase listing more items: ز دینار 'of dinars' then وز (a poetic combining particle 'و'+'ز' used for rhythm) جامه ششتری 'Shushtari garments/robes'. Again the preposition repeats for poetic effect and the nouns are coordinated.
segments.s239-g103: پرستار here denotes attendants/keepers (contextually 'attendants'); با introduces accompanying items 'with a helmet and earring(s)'. The nouns افسر (helmet) and گوشوار (earring) are objects of با, forming an instrumental/attendant description — 'attendants wearing helmets and earrings.'
segments.s239-g104: This is a descriptive apposition: همان 'those/same' + جعد مویان 'curl‑haired' (جعد 'curl' + مویان 'hairs', a compound adjective) followed by سیمین عذار 'silver‑cheeked maidens' (سیمین 'silvery' modifying عذار 'maidens'). Word order groups adjectival modifiers after the demonstrative, describing the attendants.
segments.s239-g49: نشست is a past tense verb here meaning 'sat' (verb before the subject, a common poetic order). از بر + تخت زر شهریار is a prepositional phrase describing location: بر = 'on/upon' governs تخت ('throne'), زر qualifies تخت as 'golden', and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb.
segments.s239-g50: بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'near/close by'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'him'; the name رستم follows and is modified by the adjective نامدار 'renowned' placed after the name (epithet). Word order is adverb + pronoun + name + epithet.
segments.s239-g51: A simple coordination of proper names joined by و ('and'); each name is a separate coordinate element with the final one concluding the list.
segments.s239-g52: نشستند is the 3rd person plural past 'they sat' (note: the supplied sense list for this word does not match the verbal use, so the exact dictionary index is unavailable). با is the preposition 'with', نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'the renowned' (used substantively), and نیو here is the instrument name 'ney' (reed‑flute) — together meaning 'they sat with the famed ney‑players.'
segments.s239-g53: سخن is the object 'a word/speech' (here 'speech' or 'he spoke'); گفت is the past 'said/spoke' (the provided sense entries are nominal and do not match the verbal use, so index is not available); کیخسرو is the subject. از marks the topic 'about/of' (introducing the matter), and رزمگاه means 'battlefield'. The order places the object/topic before the verb in poetic style.
segments.s239-g54: ازان is a deictic/demonstrative particle 'of that / from that / regarding that' linking back to the previously mentioned battlefield; رنج here means 'hardship/suffering'; پیگار is 'battle/struggle'; توران specifies the place/people 'Turan' and سپاه is 'army' — together 'that hardship and struggle of Turan's army.'
segments.s239-g55: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' marking the indirect object; گفت is the past 'said' (the provided senses do not reflect the verbal use, so index is unavailable); گودرز is the speaker's name; کای is a vocative particle 'O' (addressing a king) and شهریار is the addressee 'king' — together 'Goudarz said to him, "O king..."'.
segments.s239-g56: سخنها is the plural noun 'words/talks' ('there is much to say'); درازست is a predicative adjective 'is long' (here meaning 'it will take long / there is much to tell'); زین is an archaic contraction equivalent to 'از این' ('about/from this') and links to کارزار 'battle' — literally 'the words are long about this battle.' Note: the supplied senses for زین do not include this demonstrative use, hence index unavailable.
segments.s239-g105: طبقهای زرین is a noun phrase: طبقهای (plural ‘trays/plates’) is modified by the adjective زرین (‘golden’). پر از is an adjectival phrase meaning ‘full of’ where پر (‘full’) takes the content marked by از (‘of/from’); مشک و عود are the coordinated contents (‘musk and oud’). The whole segment is a single noun phrase listing an item and its contents.
segments.s239-g106: دو نعلین زرین: numeral دو precedes the noun نعلین (‘sandals’ / ‘shoes’) and the adjective زرین follows the noun, giving ‘two golden sandals.’ The conjunction و links this to زرین عمود, where زرین (here placed before عمود) again qualifies the noun ‘pillar/column’—poetic word order allows the adjective to appear before or after the noun.
segments.s239-g107: بافته is a past-participial adjective ‘woven’ modifying گوهر (‘jewel’); گوهر شاهوار is a noun + adjective ‘a regal/lofty jewel’ (شاهوار qualifies گوهر). The item is described as گوهر بافته (‘jewel-woven’). The first word برو here is archaic/poetic and does not function as the common imperative/verb ‘go’; in context it appears as a prefatory or descriptive element and is not the regular verb sense.
segments.s239-g108: چنانچون is a comparative/concessive particle ‘just as / as it was.’ بود is the past copula ‘was.’ در functions as the preposition ‘for/in’ in the idiom در خور meaning ‘fit for / worthy of’; شهریار is the noun ‘the king.’ Together چنانچون بود در خور شهریار = ‘as was fitting for the king.’
segments.s239-g109: بنزد تهمتن is a prepositional phrase ‘to (the presence of) Tahmatan’ (بنزد = ‘to/near’ + proper name تهمتن). فرستاد is the past verb ‘sent.’ شاه follows the verb as the subject (classical Persian often places the verb before its subject), so فرستاد شاه = ‘the king sent.’
segments.s239-g110: دو منزل is ‘two stages’ (منزل here = stage/station of a journey). همی is an aspectual/emphatic particle adding continuation or habitual flavour to the past verb; همی رفت = ‘he went on / went (for) two stages.’ با او is the prepositional phrase ‘with him’ and براه is an adverbial ‘on the road / en route.’ Word order places numeral + noun first, then the verbal phrase, then the accompanying prepositional phrases.
segments.s239-g111: چو = ‘when’; خسرو is the subject name; غمی شد is a verbal phrase ‘became sorrowful’ (غمی = grief, شد = became). ز marks the cause/source ‘from / because of’; راه دراز = ‘the long road/journey’ (راه = road, دراز = long). The whole clause gives the temporal/circumstantial setting: ‘when Khosrow became sorrowful from the long road.’
segments.s239-g112: فرود آمد is a light-verb construction meaning ‘came down / alighted’ (فرود = descent/alighting, آمد = came) and functions as the main action of the subject. The conjunction و links the next clause. In برد رستم نماز the verb برد (classical/poetic usage of بردن) appears before its subject رستم (common epic word order: V + S) and نماز is the object; here برد carries the sense ‘led/took/performed (the prayer),’ an older transitive use of بردن.
segments.s239-g17: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here functions as a conjunction introducing the consequent action ('so that' / 'that'), not a temporal 'until'. پیل بردند پیش is a short clause with the past plural بردند ('they carried/brought') taking پیل ('elephant') as its object and پیش as an adverb 'forward/in front'. The whole segment expresses 'he ordered that they bring the elephant forward.'
segments.s239-g18: بجنبید is an older/poetic past form meaning 'moved/stirred' (the initial ب- appears in Ferdowsi's style); the subject follows the verb in inverted order: کیخسرو 'Kay Khosrow'. از جای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his place', where خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his/own' (here written as separate خوی + ش). The clause thus reads 'Kay Khosrow moved from his place.'
segments.s239-g19: جهانی is the subject meaning 'the world/realm'; به‌آیین is an adverbial phrase 'in accordance with rite / decorously'; شد is the past/auxiliary 'became/was'; آراسته is the predicate adjective 'adorned'. Word order (subject — adverbial — verb — predicate) is typical poetic Persian where شد gives the resultive sense 'was adorned.'
segments.s239-g20: This is an asyndetic/poetic list linked to the previous clause (what the realm was adorned with). می‌و and رود etc. are coordinated nouns: می‌و (here used as the poetic/archaic 'fruit' → contextually 'wine/banquet item'), رود (here the musical sense, a stringed instrument), رامشگر 'comforter' (an agent or soothing presence), and خواسته 'the desired / desire'. The verb is omitted because the list continues the predication of the prior clause.
segments.s239-g21: تبیره is the noun (here referring to a sounded cry or announcement in context); برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'; ز هر جای is the prepositional phrase 'from every place/quarter'; و نای appends 'and the ney (reed flute)'. The order is verb then source phrase (poetic inversion) plus coordinated noun.
segments.s239-g22: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; شاه جهان is the compound subject 'king of the world'; اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'entered / came in / stepped forward' (اندر = in/into + آمد = came); ز جای marks origin 'from (his) place'. The clause is a temporal subordinate introduced by چو ('when the king came').
segments.s239-g23: همه modifies روی to mean 'the whole / entire surface'; روی پیل 'the surface (or side) of the elephant' uses juxtaposition to indicate the elephant's surface; از کران تا کران is an idiomatic range 'from edge to edge / from brim to brim' with تا meaning 'to / as far as' in this comparative use. Word order places the quantifier before the noun phrase.
segments.s239-g24: پر here is predicative 'full (of)'; از مشک specifies the filling 'of musk'; بود is the past copula 'was'; و می‌و زعفران is a coordinated noun phrase 'and (of) meyv / fruit (poetic ~ wine) and saffron' continuing the list of what the surface was full of. The noun order and omission of an extra verb are typical poetic compression.
segments.s239-g57: This is a three‑item list (می‌و، جام، آرام) followed by the modal verb باید and the adverb نخست. Word order is item(s) → باید → adverb, so the sense is 'these things are necessary first.' می‌و is an archaic/poetic form (here meaning wine/produce) used in parallel with جام (cup).
segments.s239-g58: پس آنگاه is a doubled temporal sequence ('then, afterwards') used for emphasis. ازین کار is از + این (here written ین) meaning 'about/of this matter.' پرسی is the present/subjunctive (or hortative) form 'you should ask' (not a simple past), and درست is an adverb/adjective meaning 'properly/correctly', modifying the action.
segments.s239-g59: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/laid' and takes خوان (banquet/table) as its object. The clause then continues with و and an intransitive past verb بخندید followed by its subject شاه (verb–subject order is common in epic Persian), so 'and the king laughed.'
segments.s239-g60: که here introduces an explanatory clause 'for/because.' ناهار بودی is a past-state clause 'it was noon' (همانا adds emphasis 'truly' or 'indeed'), and به راه is a locative phrase 'on the road' (they were traveling), with به used in the sense 'on/towards' + راه 'road.'
segments.s239-g61: بخوان here functions as a lexical noun/verb meaning 'recital/singing' or 'one who sings/recites' and combines with the compound verb بر می‌آورد ('brought/caused to be brought'); بر is part of the verbal compound برآوردن. The clause ends with و رامشگران starting the next clause; رامشگران is a plural agent noun ('peacemakers' / comforters / entertainers) introducing who will act next.
segments.s239-g62: بپرسش is a verbal-noun/inflected form meaning 'asking/questioning' and with گرفت (past 'took') forms a light‑verb construction 'took up questioning / began to ask.' از کران تا کران is an idiomatic prepositional range 'from edge to edge' (از ... تا ... expresses extent).
segments.s239-g63: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'about/from' and starts a list of topics. افراسیاب is a proper name. وز in the manuscript is actually a contraction of و + ز ('and from'), so its printed lexical senses (gust/weight) do not fit here; it should be read as 'and from'. پولادوند is an epithet/name (literally 'steel‑something'), used here as a name.
segments.s239-g64: The second half repeats the preposition (ز/از) to mean 'about/from the ship (کشتی) and about/from the تابداده کمند.' تابداده is a compound adjective (تاب‑داده) meaning 'twisted/spun' or 'strengthened', qualifying کمند 'lasso/snare'. The phrase lists further topics of questioning.
segments.s239-g1: فعل گذشته یافت (3pl/3sg here) follows its subject as is common in classical Persian; the noun شتر is the direct object/thing found. The reduplicated چندان و چندان intensifies quantity ('so many, so many'), and گله (here a herd/flock) pairs with شتر to list what was found.
segments.s239-g2: که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that/that'), followed by the prepositional phrase از بارگی ('by/from the burden-bearing' — i.e. by virtue of having beasts to carry baggage). شد is the past-tense copular/terminative 'became' and سپه is the grammatical subject in the predicate construction سپه بی‌گله, where بی‌گله is a predicative adjective meaning 'without complaint' or 'untroubled'.
segments.s239-g3: ز توران is a locative/prepositional phrase 'from Turan' placed before the subject سپه (the host). برنهادند is an archaic past plural (3pl) of برنهادن meaning 'to raise/take up' or idiomatically 'to set out' and takes رخت (baggage/gear) as its object; the verb therefore expresses departure with baggage.
segments.s239-g4: This is a noun series serving as the object/details of رخت: سلیح (=weapons) modified postpositively by the adjective گرانمایه ('precious'), followed by a coordinated pair تاج و تخت ('crown and throne'). Persian commonly places adjectives after the noun, and the items are linked by و.
segments.s239-g5: خروش آمد is a simple nominal clause ('a roar came/was heard') with آمد as the intransitive past 'arose'. The following noun phrase ناله گاودم is coordinated by the preceding و and stands as a second sound/image ('and the moan/lowing of the oxen'); classical and poetic Persian often strings such nominal clauses together without an extra verb.
segments.s239-g6: جرس is the direct object 'bell' and برکشیدند is a past plural verb 'they lifted/raised' (here idiomatically 'they sounded/ran[g]'); the coordinating و links the next noun phrase رویینه خم, where رویینه is an adjective 'armored, of iron' modifying خم ('a curve/curved thing'), so the clause lists what they raised or displayed (bells and curved iron objects).
segments.s239-g7: سوی شهر ایران is a prepositional phrase 'toward the city of Iran' placed before the verb. نهادند is the past-tense 3pl of نهادن 'to place/set', and روی ('face') is the direct object here; the idiom روی نهادن (to set one's face toward) means 'to head toward'. Word order is typical: goal phrase + verb + object.
segments.s239-g8: This noun phrase describes the army: سپاهی 'the host/army' followed by بران گونه ('of that kind' or 'in such a manner'), where بران is a demonstrative-like element and گونه is 'manner/kind'. The postpositional phrase با رنگ و بوی ('with colour and scent') adds descriptive flourish — idiomatically indicating showy appearance or splendour.
segments.s239-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking the source/position. افسر (helmet) is the head-ornament, followed by the possessor phrase سر پیلبان ('the head of the mahout') in post‑nominal order (helmet + head + possessor). پرنگار is an adjectival/postpositive qualifier 'richly ornamented' modifying افسر. The whole chunk therefore means 'the richly ornamented helmet on the mahout's head.'
segments.s239-g26: ز again is the preposition 'from' or 'hanging from'. گوش اندر آویخته is a participial phrase: گوش (ear) + اندر (at/in) + آویخته (hung, past participle) — 'hung at the ear'. گوشوار (ear-ornament/earring) is the head noun. Together: 'an earring hung from the ear.'
segments.s239-g27: بسی functions as an adverb/quantifier 'much' or 'many' modifying the following objects. زعفران و درم is the coordinated object ('saffron and dirhams/coins'). ریختند is a third‑person plural past verb 'they poured/scattered'. Word order is object(s) then verb (typical Persian SOV).
segments.s239-g28: ز بر is a compound prepositional idea 'upon' or 'over'; مشک و عنبر are the coordinated nouns 'musk and amber(gris)'. همی is the old/poetic imperfective marker giving an iterative/ongoing flavour to the action and here precedes the past verb. بیختند is the past plural verb meaning 'they scattered/strewn' (the exact lexeme is archaic here). The phrase means 'and upon [them] they likewise scattered musk and amber.'
segments.s239-g29: همه شهر is the subject phrase 'the whole city'. آوای رامشگران is a genitive construction 'the sound/voice (آوا) of the رامشگران' — here 'رامشگران' are the entertainers/musicians/comforters; the noun آوا is possessed by that group. This chunk sets up 'the whole city [the] sound of the entertainers...'.
segments.s239-g30: نشسته is a past participle used predicatively meaning 'is/was settled' or 'filled' (i.e., 'resounded with / was full of'). ز هر سو is an adverbial 'from every side'. کران تا کران is an idiomatic adverbial 'from edge to edge' (تا here 'to / as far as'). Together they specify extent: 'was filled from every side, from edge to edge.'
segments.s239-g31: چنان is the adverb 'so' / 'thus' that modifies the whole clause. بد here is a poetic/contracted form (in classical verse it functions as a past copula like بود 'was') — it should be read as the past‑state marker 'was'. جهان را has the object/topic marking را. ز شادی و داد is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/with joy and justice' where داد is the noun 'justice'. The clause then reads 'Thus was the world from joy and justice...' (i.e. the world was so, by virtue of joy and justice).
segments.s239-g32: که introduces a result clause 'that'. گیتی روان را is the topical/object phrase (گیتی = the world/earth, روان here conveys 'animated/moving' or 'living'); را marks it as definite/topic. دوامست is the fused form 'دوام است' (it endures / is lasting) in third person; و شاد adds a coordinated predicate 'and is joyful.' The clause therefore states the result: 'that the world endures and is glad.'
segments.s239-g73: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” افگندن is the infinitival/masdar form “the throwing down” (here functioning as a noun). دیو is the object “the demon.” وز here is a fused preposition/conjunction (equivalent to و ز = “and from”), linking a second nominal action. کشتنش is the masdar/infinitive “killing him,” with the clitic -ش referring back to the demon. Together the phrase lists two sources/actions introduced by ز ... وز (“from X and from Y”).
segments.s239-g74: همان is the demonstrative/adverb “that very” or “indeed.” جنگ is the noun “war/battle.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” پیگار is a synonym meaning “battle/struggle.” و again links the next noun. کین is the noun “vengeance/hatred,” and جستنش is the masdar/infinitive “seeking it” (the -ش referring back to the vengeance), so the whole line names the character of the action: “that was war, battle, and the seeking of vengeance.”
segments.s239-g75: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” افتاد is past tense “fell” (3rd sg.). بر خاک is the prepositional phrase “onto the earth.” ز here (written as زو in the line) is the source marker “from (him).” و is the simple conjunction “and.” رفت هوش is an idiomatic verbal phrase “his awareness went” → “he lost consciousness”; رفت is the past of رفتن meaning “go/vanish” and هوش is “awareness/sense.”
segments.s239-g76: برآمد is the past “arose/rose up.” ز is the preposition “from.” گردان is a collective noun “troop/ranks.” دیوان here refers to the demonic host (the plural/collective sense) and is the source of the sound. خروش is the noun “roar/cry/uproar”; word order yields “a cry arose from the troop of demons.”
segments.s239-g77: چو “when.” آمد is the past “came.” بهوش (بَه‌هوش) literally “to/into sense” — آمد بهوش = “came to consciousness” (regained consciousness). آن سرافراز دیو is the noun phrase “that proud/triumphant demon” serving as the subject. The clause thus means “when that proud demon regained consciousness.”
segments.s239-g78: برآمد is “arose/issued forth.” بناگاه is an adverb “suddenly.” ز (زو) is the source “from him.” یک is the numeral/article “one/a,” and غریو is “a shout/clamor.” The order is poetic but straightforward: “suddenly a shout arose from him.”
segments.s239-g79: همانگه is an adverb “at that moment/instantly.” درآمد is past “entered/appeared” (here used with the following instrumental phrase). باسپ is the fused older form of با اسب (“with the horse/onto the horse” — i.e. taking or mounting a horse) and functions as the accompanying phrase. و is the conjunction “and.” برفت is past “departed/rode off” (بر + رفتن). Together: “At that moment he took (mounted) the horse and rode away.”
segments.s239-g80: همی is an adverb here meaning roughly “then/just so.” بند جانش is a genitive compound “the bond/tether of his life” (بند = bond/fetter, جانش = his life). ز رستم is the agent/instrumental phrase “by/from Rostam.” بکفت is an archaic/past verbal form meaning here that the bond was snapped/severed (i.e. his life was cut short). So the clause reports that Rostam broke the bond of his life (he killed him).
segments.s239-g9: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آگاهی آمد is a simple past clause 'information/news came', with آمد as past of آمدن. ز رستم is a poetic/contracted prepositional phrase 'from/about Rostam' (ز = از). بشاه is an archaic orthographic contraction of به شاه, i.e. a prepositional phrase 'to the king' (marks the recipient of the news).
segments.s239-g10: خروش آمد is a noun + past verb 'a clamor/roar came/rose' (standard Persian order: noun then verb). از شهر is the source 'from the city'. وز here functions poetically as a contraction of و + ز (i.e. 'and from'); it links equally to بارگاه 'the court/palace'.
segments.s239-g11: از ایران gives the origin 'from Iran'. تبیره is a nominal 'report/interpretation' (classical usage here = a rumour or announcement). برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'. بابر is presented as a proper name (the item/word of the report); word order places the report's label after the verb.
segments.s239-g12: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/namely' giving the content of the report. آمد is past 'came/arrived' (here used with intransitive sense). خداوند functions as an honorific 'lord/sovereign' attached to the following names; گوپال و ببر are two coordinated names/epithets supplied as the parties who have come. The conjunction و simply coordinates the two names.
segments.s239-g13: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (here introducing a singular event). شادمانی is the noun 'rejoicing/joy'. بد here is not its ordinary 'bad' sense; in classical poetic usage it functions as an intensifier ('mighty/great') — this sense is not among the supplied literal senses. اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world'.
segments.s239-g14: خنیده is a perfect/passive participle meaning 'sung/chantered' (here describing how the rejoicing is expressed). میان means 'among/between' and governs the following coordinated nouns. کهان و مهان is a poetic pair (literally 'the world/ancients' and 'guests'); in context it means 'chiefs/nobles and guests' or 'notables and visitors' — the phrase marks the social circle among whom the song/joy was heard.
segments.s239-g15: دل شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's heart' (noun + possessor). شد is past 'became'. چون here is the simile marker 'like/as'. بهشت برین is a compound 'the highest/paradisal paradise' — برین is the superlative/adjectival formation (classical suffixal form) making 'the supreme paradise'.
segments.s239-g16: همی is the classical continuous/habitual prefix meaning 'kept on/was continually'. خواند is past third‑person singular of خواندن and here means 'called out/uttered' (used for vocal praise). بر کردگار آفرین is the object/phrase he utters: بر = 'to/upon', کردگار = 'the Creator/Lord', and آفرین is an exclamatory acclamation 'praise/bravo' used here as 'Praise be to...'.
segments.s239-g65: This is a short reported‑speech frame: بدو (to him) is the indirect object placed before the speech verb گفت (said). کای شهریار is a vocative phrase introduced by کای (an archaic vocative particle 'O'), addressing the king; word order is "[to him] said Goudarz, 'O king...'".
segments.s239-g66: نزاید is the classical present (with negative prefix ن‑) of زادن 'to be born' meaning 'is/does not come into being'; ز مادر = 'from (a) mother' uses poetic preposition ز = 'from'. چو + noun makes a simile 'like/as', so چو رستم سوار = 'like Rostam the rider/horseman.' Overall: 'is not born from a mother like Rostam the horseman.'
segments.s239-g67: This begins a conditional clause. اگر ... آید = 'if ... comes'; پیش آید = 'comes forward/appears'. The poet repeats the conditional idea with ار (= 'if/supposing') before the second noun as a stylistic/poetic alternative. Word order is typical: condition marker (اگر) + subject (دیو) + verb (آید).
segments.s239-g68: ز چنگ درازش = 'from/ out of his long claw' where -ش is the possessive suffix 'his/its' on دراز ('long'); چنگ here means 'claw/talon' (not 'harp'). نیابد is the classical negative present of یافتن 'to obtain/attain' meaning 'does not obtain' or 'cannot get'; رها ('release/freedom') is the object: literally 'does not obtain release' = 'does not escape/ be freed.'
segments.s239-g69: هزار افرین باد is an optative formula: هزار 'a thousand' quantifies the noun افرین 'praise', and باد is the optative particle meaning 'may (it be)'. بر + شهریار = a prepositional phrase 'upon the king' (بر = upon; شهریار = king). Word order places the wish first, then its target.
segments.s239-g70: بویژه 'especially' narrows the focus. بر is the same preposition 'upon/to'. ین here represents the orthographic variant of این 'this' (so برین = 'upon this'). شیردل is an adjectival epithet 'lion‑hearted', and نامدار means 'renowned/illustrious'. The phrase modifies which person the blessing especially applies to.
segments.s239-g71: بگفت is a past/reporting verb 'he said/recounted'. آنچ 'that which/what' introduces the content of his recounting as a relative/object clause. کرد is the past tense of 'to do' and takes او 'he' as its subject. بپولادوند is the name/epithet with an attached b‑ (here functioning as the prepositional prefix 'به/بـ' = 'to/against' or simply orthographically attached to the name), so the clause means 'he said what he had done to Polādvand.'
segments.s239-g72: ز is the preposition 'from/through' marking means or manner. The line lists paired means joined by coordinating particles: کشتی 'wrestling' (a combat form), و 'and', نیرنگ 'stratagem/trick', then وز here functions as 'و + ز' ('and from') linking the next pair; رنگ is 'appearance/feint' (an artifice or guise), و 'and', بند 'bond/fetter' (capture). Together the phrase specifies the ways in which the action was done.
segments.s239-g113: ورا کرد پدرود و ز ایران برفت: 'ورا' is an old/poetic third‑person indirect object meaning 'to him' (i.e. 'to that person'); 'کرد' is the past of 'کردن' used as the light verb 'did/made' (here, 'bade'); 'پدرود' is a noun meaning 'farewell'. The coordinating 'و' links the two clauses; 'ز ایران' is the prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). The final word here is written 'برفت' (past), an archaic/poetic past of 'رفتن' meaning 'departed' — the normal modern form would be 'بَرَفت'/'رفت', but the exact lexical sense for the provided token is not available in the sense list, so that sense is marked -1.
segments.s239-g114: سوی زابلستان خرامید تفت: 'سوی زابلستان' is a directional phrase 'toward Zabulistan' (سوی = 'toward(s)'; زابلستان = proper place name). 'خرامید' is the past tense of the verb 'خرامیدن' meaning 'he strode/ambled proudly' (a poetic motion verb). The final word 'تفت' here functions as a proper name (the subject performing the striding), placed after the verb for poetic word order; the provided senses for that token do not match this proper‑name reading, so its sense is given as -1.
segments.s239-g115: سراسر جهان گشت بر شاه راست: 'سراسر جهان' is the subject phrase 'the whole world'. 'گشت' is the past of 'گشتن' used as 'turned' or 'was turned'. 'بر شاه راست' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the king's side' or 'in favor of the king' (بر = 'toward/on'; شاه = 'king'; راست here indicates the 'right/side' or 'right-hand/true side' in a figurative sense). Note the verb precedes the prepositional complement in this poetic line, which is common in classical Persian.
segments.s239-g116: همی گشت گیتی بران سان که خواست: 'همی' is an archaic/poetic adverb signaling continued or habitual action ('still/kept on'); 'گشت' again is 'turned/was turning'. 'گیتی' = 'the world' (synonymous with جهان). 'بران سان' = 'in/onto that sort of way' ('بران' = 'upon/thereon', 'سان' = 'such/like'), and the relative clause 'که خواست' follows ('that/which it desired' — خواست is past of خواستن 'to will/ask'). Overall, the clause says the world continued to move/turn in that manner as it wished.
segments.s239-g117: سر آوردم این رزم کاموس نیز: 'سر آوردن' is a classical compound verb meaning 'to bring to a head/finish' — here in past first‑person 'آوردم' ('I have brought/ended'). Grammatically the noun 'سر' combines with 'آوردن' to express 'finish'; none of the isolated senses listed for 'سر' fit that compound use, so its sense is marked -1. 'این رزم کاموس' is the direct object 'this battle of Kāmus' (کاموس is a proper name here), and 'نیز' means 'also/too'.
segments.s239-g118: درازست و کم نیست زو یک پشیز: 'درازست' is a predicative adjective phrase 'it is long' (the copular 'است' is incorporated in the adjective form). 'و' = 'and' links the clauses. 'کم نیست' literally 'is not little' → idiomatically 'not lacking' or 'there is no smallness'; 'زو' = 'from it' (poetic contraction of 'ز' + 'او'), and 'یک پشیز' = 'one pashiz' (a tiny bit/trifle). Together the second clause says 'and it is not lacking even a trifle from it' (i.e. it is ample/considerable). Note the dataset split the particle 'زو' into components; the second listed 'و' token in the data does not represent the coordinating 'and' here and so is marked -1 in senses.
segments.s239-g119: گر از داستان یک سخن کم بدی: 'گر' is the classical conditional 'if'. 'از داستان' = 'from the tale/account'. 'یک سخن' = 'one word/line/phrase'. 'کم بدی' here means 'you give/make it less' i.e. 'you omit/reduce it' — grammatically 'کم' = 'little/less' and 'بدی' is a second‑person past/future‑conditional form of a light verb meaning 'you would give/do' in older usage; the lexical senses provided for 'بدی' in the list do not match this verbal usage, so that token is marked -1.
segments.s239-g120: روان مرا جای ماتم بدی: 'روان' = 'soul/spirit'. The phrase 'روانِ مرا' (here written with the pronoun after the noun, as is common in classical Persian) functions as 'my soul' — the data split 'مرا' into elements; the token shown as 'م' in the list is actually part of the clitic 'مرا' ('me/my') and so the provided lemma/sense is not applicable (marked -1). 'را' is the object/definite marker attached to 'مرا' here by the tokenization. 'جای ماتم' = 'a place of mourning' (جای = place; ماتم = mourning), and again 'بدی' is the verb 'you would make/give' in classical usage, so its lexical sense from the provided list does not apply and is marked -1. The full clause therefore means 'you would make my soul a place of mourning.'

sentences.s239-s61: My heart rejoiced at Polādvand, who added a steel link to the chain of steel.
sentences.s239-s45: For a week he remained with a cup in his hand, rejoicing; he took the crown and the throne and sat (upon it).
sentences.s239-s46: They began speaking of Rostam’s deeds and then sang/declared an ode to the hero.
sentences.s239-s47: Tahmatan stayed near the king for a month, holding a cup in the royal presence.
sentences.s239-s48: After that he said to the king: 'O honored, learned, renowned crowned one.'
sentences.s239-s21: He ordered that Piltan be seated; then, taking him by the hand, he led him all the way.
sentences.s239-s22: He (kept) said, 'Why did you linger so long — that you would fling fire upon us?';
sentences.s239-s23: As Tūs and Fariburz and Goudarz and Giv, as Roham and Gorgin and the troop of Neyu,
sentences.s239-s24: From the road toward the iwan of the king they came; they came to that renowned court.
sentences.s239-s17: When Tahmatan saw the lofty crown, he saw the whole world filled with acclaim.
sentences.s239-s18: He descended and brought the prayer before him; Khosrow asked from the long road.
sentences.s239-s19: He seized him into his embrace within the king's close hold; thus there was a pause until some time passed.
sentences.s239-s20: The king of the world cried out 'Bravo!' and (addressed) that famed mōbed and champion.
sentences.s239-s41: He became so joyful at that merchant's words that, as is said, he lifted his head from the portico.
sentences.s239-s42: He answered thus: “O champion! you are old, awake, and clear‑minded.”
sentences.s239-s43: Let a man of wisdom be a teacher; may the turning of the world keep him in its care.
sentences.s239-s44: May the evil eye be far from this champion; may all his life be given to feasting.
sentences.s239-s49: The world‑ruler is learned and kind, but to me the face of Zal is the desire (I long for Zal's face).
sentences.s239-s50: The king of the world opened the treasure, revealing something of great worth that had been hidden.
sentences.s239-s51: Of rubies and crowns and rings; of dinars and Shushtari garments.
sentences.s239-s52: Attendants with helmets and earrings — those same curly‑haired, silver‑cheeked maidens.
sentences.s239-s25: The king sat on his golden throne; Rostam the renowned sat close beside him.
sentences.s239-s26: Fariborz and Goudarz and Roham and Giv sat with the famed ney‑players.
sentences.s239-s27: Kiykhosrow spoke of the battlefield, of that hardship and the struggle of Turan's army.
sentences.s239-s28: Goudarz said to him, 'O king, there is much to say about this battle.'
sentences.s239-s53: Golden trays filled with musk and oud, two golden sandals, and a golden pillar.
sentences.s239-s54: A jewel-woven, regal (garment), just as was fitting for the king.
sentences.s239-s55: The king sent (someone) to Tahmatan; he went with him two stages on the road.
sentences.s239-s56: When Khosrow became sorrowful from the long road, he alighted, and Rostam led (performed) the prayer.
sentences.s239-s9: He ordered that the elephant be brought forward, and Kay Khosrow moved from his place.
sentences.s239-s10: The realm was decorously adorned: wine, the lute, a comforter, and desire.
sentences.s239-s11: From every quarter rose tabireh (a cry/sound) and the ney, when the king of the world came forth from his place.
sentences.s239-s12: The whole surface of the elephant, from edge to edge, was filled with musk and saffron.
sentences.s239-s29: First one needs wine, a cup, and rest; then afterwards you should properly ask about this matter.
sentences.s239-s30: They set the banquet, and the king laughed, for it was truly noon on the road.
sentences.s239-s31: He called for a recitation/singing and brought it forth, and the peacemakers/entertainers began to put questions from edge to edge.
sentences.s239-s32: About Afrasiab and about Puladvand, about the ship and about the twisted/snared rope (lasso).
sentences.s239-s1: They found so many camels and so many herds that, by taking on the baggage, the host became without complaint.
sentences.s239-s2: From Turan the host set out, bearing precious arms, the crown, and the throne.
sentences.s239-s3: A roar arose and the moan of the oxen; they lifted/rang the bells and the iron-curved (implements).
sentences.s239-s4: They turned the face of the army toward the city of Iran — an army of that sort, with colour and scent (i.e. with splendour).
sentences.s239-s13: From the richly ornamented helmet on the mahout's head an earring hung at the ear.
sentences.s239-s14: They scattered much saffron and dirhams, and upon (them) they likewise scattered musk and amber.
sentences.s239-s15: The whole city was filled with the music (voice) of the entertainers, from every side, from edge to edge.
sentences.s239-s16: Thus the world was so (filled) with joy and justice that the world endures and is glad.
sentences.s239-s37: Throwing down the demon and killing him — that was indeed war and combat and the seeking of vengeance.
sentences.s239-s38: When he fell to the earth his consciousness went; from the ranks of the demons a cry arose.
sentences.s239-s39: When that proud demon came to his senses, suddenly a shout arose from him.
sentences.s239-s40: At that very moment he mounted (or took) his horse and rode off; in that instant the tether of his life was broken by Rostam.
sentences.s239-s5: When news of Rostam reached the king, a clamor rose from the city and from the court.
sentences.s239-s6: From Iran a report arose — 'Babur' — that the lords Gopal and Babr had come.
sentences.s239-s7: A certain great rejoicing arose in the world, chanted among chiefs and guests.
sentences.s239-s8: The king's heart became like the highest paradise; he kept calling out 'Praise to the Creator.'
sentences.s239-s33: Goudarz said to him, "O king, no mother gives birth to anyone like Rostam the horseman."
sentences.s239-s34: If a demon or a dragon comes forward, it will not escape from his long claw.
sentences.s239-s35: A thousand praises be upon the king, especially upon this renowned, lion‑hearted one.
sentences.s239-s36: He recounted what he had done to Polādvand — in wrestling and stratagem, and in feint and bonds.
sentences.s239-s57: He bade him farewell and departed from Iran; Taft strode toward Zabulistan.
sentences.s239-s58: The whole world turned to the king's side; the world continued to turn in that manner as it willed.
sentences.s239-s59: I have brought this battle of Kāmus to an end as well; it is long, and it is not lacking even a trifle.
sentences.s239-s60: If you omit one word from the tale, you would make my soul a place of mourning.
